title: The Conscious Mind
subtitle: In Search of a Fundamental Theory
author: David J. Chalmers
themes: The hard problem of consciousness — why is there subjective experience at all? — is distinct from the easy problems of explaining cognitive functions
Consciousness cannot be reductively explained in purely physical terms
Naturalistic dualism: consciousness is a fundamental feature of the universe, like mass or charge
Experience is not the same as function — explaining the function does not explain the experience
Consciousness may be a fundamental property of information, not just of biological brains
A complete theory of the universe must include consciousness as a fundamental element
excerpt: Consciousness is the most familiar and the most mysterious phenomenon. But it is also something of an embarrassment for the physical sciences. No one has the foggiest idea how or why physical processes in the brain give rise to conscious experience. This explanatory gap is the central problem of consciousness. I call it the hard problem.
